segments.s115-g169: نیاسود is a past-tense negative compound (نی- + آسود) meaning 'was not at rest / did not find repose'; it stands before its temporal complements for emphasis. تیره شب و پاک روز is a poetic pair of coordinated noun phrases ('dark night and clear/bright day') that specify the time span during which the action holds.
segments.s115-g170: همی is a classical adverbial particle meaning 'continually' / 'still' and here marks repeated or ongoing action. راند is the past 3rd-singular of راندن ('drove, rode on'). تا ... پیش is a limit expression: تا here means 'as far as / up to' and پیش + noun means 'in front of / before (the place)'.
segments.s115-g171: بدانجا که begins a locative relative clause: 'to that place where ...'. کاووس is the subject (Kavus) and لشکر کشید is a light-verb collocation meaning 'drew up / mustered an army' (here کشید = 'drew/mustered'). Note: کشید is used here in the sense 'to draw/lead (an army)', not in agricultural senses.
segments.s115-g172: ز introduces the source ('from'). دیوان جادو is a genitive/compound meaning 'the demons' (دیوان) + 'magic' (جادو) — i.e. an evil magic sent by demons. بدو ('to him') is the dative clitic phrase, بد is an adverb/adjective 'evil/ill', and رسید is the past 'reached / came to' — literal: 'from the demons, magic (evil) reached him'.
segments.s115-g173: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. یک نیمه بگذشت is a time clause meaning 'one half (i.e. half) passed' — بگذشت is past of گذشتن 'to pass' and the following از تیره شب specifies the whole of which the half is taken ('of the dark night').
segments.s115-g174: خروش آمد is a nominal subject + past verb: 'a cry/uproar came'. از دشت marks source/place ('from the plain'). و links the second noun phrase بانگ جلب, where بانگ is 'a cry/crying sound' and جلب here names the character of the cry (a summons or call to attract/gather).
segments.s115-g175: به مازندران indicates the location/target ('in / towards Mazandaran'). آتش is the object 'fire' and افروختند is the past 3rd-plural of افروختن 'they kindled / lit', so the clause means 'they lit fires in Mazandaran.'
segments.s115-g176: به هر جای = 'in every place' (به + هر + جای). شمعی here denotes 'a candle' (an indefinite/candle at each place) and همی سوختند is past plural with the classical continuous particle همی ('candles burned everywhere' / 'a candle burned in each place').
segments.s115-g1: وزانجا is an adverbial phrase 'and/from there' placed at the start. سوی راه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the road.' بنهاد is an archaic past third-person singular of نهادن 'to place/set,' and روی (literally 'face') is the object placed after the verb; the word order is poetic/inverted (adverbial – prepositional phrase – verb – object) but reads: 'he set his face toward the road.'
segments.s115-g2: چنان چون ... بود is a comparative phrase meaning 'just as' or 'such as'; بود is the past copula 'was.' مردم راه‌جوی is the noun phrase 'the road-seeking people' (subject) that follows the predicate in this poetic order, so the clause means 'just as the travelers were.'
segments.s115-g3: همی رفت is an iterative/imperfective past marker plus past verb used in epic style — 'kept going' or 'was going.' پویان is a participial noun 'the seekers' (subject). به جایی رسید is a normal verbal phrase 'reached a place' with رسید past tense 'arrived/reached.' Note the verb preceding or adjacent to the subject is common in older/Persian poetry.
segments.s115-g4: که introduces a relative/locative clause 'where/that.' اندر جهان is a locative 'in the world.' روشنایی ندید is negative past: ندید = 'did not see' (here impersonal or with omitted subject), so the clause means 'where no light was seen' or 'where there was no light.'
segments.s115-g5: شب تیره = 'dark night' (adjective after noun). چون introduces a simile 'like/as,' and روی زنگی سیاه is the simile's ground: 'the face of a Zangi who is black/dark-skinned.' Word order places the descriptive noun phrase after چون for poetic effect.
segments.s115-g6: This line uses correlative negation 'نه ... نه' = 'neither ... nor.' ستاره نه پیدا means 'no star was visible' (پیدا = 'visible/appeared'), followed by نه خورشید و ماه 'nor sun nor moon.' The copula is omitted; the adjectives/predicates follow the noun in poetic order.
segments.s115-g7: تو خورشید گفتی: گفتی here is the past second-person 'you said' introducing reported speech. The following clause 'به بند اندرست' is the content of that utterance. به بند = 'in a bond/fetter' (به marks the locative), and اندرست is an archaic present/state-form 'is within' (copula-like), so the clause reads 'you said the sun is in bonds.'
segments.s115-g8: ستاره (the stars) is the subject. به خم کمند is a prepositional phrase 'in the curve/bend of the snare' (به + خم + کمند), with خم 'bend/curve' governed by کمند 'snare/lasso.' اندرست again is the older-state verb 'is within,' so the clause means 'the stars are in the bend of a snare.' The structure is a short copular clause with poetic inversion (locative phrase before the verb).
segments.s115-g25: بخفت is a 3rd-person singular past form (archaic/poetic) of خفتن with the prefixed b-: 'he slept.' و simply links clauses ('and/but'). بیاسود is a predicative adjective/compound (بی + آسود) meaning 'not at rest / not comforted,' so the pair بخفت و بیاسود contrasts sleeping with lack of repose. از introduces the source/reason ('from/of'), رنج تن = 'the suffering of the body' (رنج = noun 'suffering'; تن = 'body').
segments.s115-g26: هم ... هم is a coordinating emphatic pairing 'both ... and / also ... and'; the first هم marks another source: از رخش غم بد = 'from his steed (رخش) [came] bad/bitter grief' where از = 'from', غم = 'sorrow', and بد qualifies it. The second هم + از خویشتن adds 'and also from himself' (خویشتن = 'himself'). The verbs are understood from the previous clause (ellipsis of 'he was not at rest').
segments.s115-g27: چو = 'when' (temporal). در سبزه is a locative phrase 'in the greenery / in the grass.' دید is 3rd-person past 'saw.' اسپ را is the definite object (اسپ 'steed/horse' + را object marker), and دشتوان is the subject 'the people of the plain / rustics.' The object and locative precede the subject and verb in poetic word order.
segments.s115-g28: گشاده‌زبان is an adjective 'outspoken / fluent of tongue' describing the person who acts. سوی او = 'toward him.' شد here is the past auxiliary/verb 'became / did' and is followed by دوان (a verbal form 'ran / rushed'), so شد دوان conveys 'ran toward him' (poetic periphrasis where شد + present-stem indicates action).
segments.s115-g29: سوی = 'toward.' رستم و رخش are the goals 'Rostam and Rakhsh.' بنهاد is a past verb 'put/placed' used idiomatically with روی to mean 'directed/turned (his) face.' روی here is 'face' (the thing turned). The verb precedes/follows nouns in the flexible poetic order.
segments.s115-g30: یکی is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one' (subject). چوب زد is verb + object (object 'chub' 'stick' placed before زد 'struck'); گرم here functions adverbially 'forcefully/vehemently.' بر پای اوی is a prepositional phrase 'on/against his foot' with اوی an archaic possessive/clitic meaning 'his.'
segments.s115-g31: چو = 'when.' از خواب = 'from sleep.' بیدار شد is a two-word past: بیدار 'awake' + شد 'became' = 'awoke.' پیلتن is placed after the verb as the subject (poetic inversion); it is a proper name/place here.
segments.s115-g32: بدو = 'to him' (dative prepositional form). دشتوان is the subject 'the rustics/people of the plain.' گفت is used as the past verb 'said' (the sense 'said' is the intended meaning though that exact gloss isn't in the provided sense list). کای is the vocative 'O!' and اهرمن is the addressee 'Ahriman' (the devil), so the clause reports them addressing him.
segments.s115-g9: عنان (reins) is the thing given and comes before the verb; رخش را uses the enclitic را to mark the definite noun (the steed) associated with the action. داد is the simple past 'gave'. بنهاد is the past of بنهادن 'to set/place/turn' and here combines with روی ('face/head/direction') so the clause means 'he handed the reins and turned/set its head'. Word order is typical classical Persian: objects and modifiers can precede the verb; the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s115-g10: نه ... نه ... is the paired negation 'neither ... nor ...'. دید is the past 'saw/beheld'. از here is the preposition 'from', so the phrase means 'he saw neither a height nor a stream from the darkness' (سیاهی = 'darkness/blackness'). The order places the negators before the nouns as is common in poetic prose.
segments.s115-g11: وزانجا is the fused 'and/from there' (و + زانجا) linking with the previous action; سوی + روشنایی = 'toward the light'. رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived'. This is a straightforward adverbial + directional phrase followed by a past-tense verb.
segments.s115-g12: زمین is 'the earth/ground' and پرنیان functions adjectivally as 'silken' (lit. 'silken robe(s)'); دید is past 'saw/beheld'. و یکسر خوید links with a verb خوید (archaic) — here 'یکسر' = 'entirely/altogether' and خوید is an archaic verb meaning roughly 'felt/was moved' (or 'took delight'), so the clause means 'he saw the earth clothed in silk and was wholly moved/delighted.'
segments.s115-g13: جهانی here denotes 'the world' (a nominative/theme); ز is the poetic preposition 'from', پیری 'old age', شده is the past participle 'become/been made', and نوجوان is 'young/youthful'. The phrase literally reads 'a world made young from old age' — an instance of the perfect/resultative 'شده + adjective'.
segments.s115-g14: همه 'all/entirely' modifies the following nouns; سبزه = 'green growth/greens' and آبهای = 'waters' (plural with the ezāfe omitted in poetry), روان here is adjectival 'flowing' (not 'soul'). The sequence lists the renewed features of the world: 'all green and flowing waters.'
segments.s115-g15: همه 'all' quantifies جامه 'garments'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. The form برش in classical usage here functions as 'on him/over him' (a fused/archaic locative/pronominal use), so the literal reading is 'all garments upon him were like water.' چون آب بود = 'were like water' (چون = 'as/like', بود = past of 'to be').
segments.s115-g16: نیازش is 'his need' (نیاز + possessive -ش); به is the preposition 'for/to', آسایش = 'comfort/ease', و = 'and', خواب = 'sleep'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause states what his (present) requirement or desire was: 'his need was for comfort and sleep.'
segments.s115-g49: برفتم is an archaic/past-form verb meaning “I went/left” (poetic بر+رفت). که introduces a purpose clause here (“so that / in order that”), linking to the subjunctive برانم. اسپش = اسپ + ـش (possessive) “his steed.” برانم is a present-subjunctive form of راندن meaning “I may drive/ride.” ز is the poetic contraction of از = “from,” and کشت here means cultivated land/field (not ‘killing’).
segments.s115-g50: مرا = me + object marker (م + را) is the direct object. خود is an emphatic reflexive meaning “himself.” به before اسپ and before کشته is a preposition used here like “onto/toward” (poetic). کشته means “the slain/corpse” and نهشت is an archaic past of ‘to place/put’ (he set/laid). The clause thus says someone himself put the speaker on the horse and among the slain.
segments.s115-g51: مرا (object) + دید (3rd-person past) = “he saw me.” برجست is an archaic past meaning “he sprang up / rose” or “appeared.” و links actions. یافه (past/found) is used adverbially “having found” or “on finding (me).” نگفت is the negative past of گفت (“he did not say / he said nothing”).
segments.s115-g52: دو گوشم = “my two ears” (دو + گوش + possessive ـم). بکند here is an older/polite past-form (from کندن or کردن in poetic usage) meaning “he pulled/tore off” or more generally “he did (it).” و همانجا = “and thereupon / right there.” بخفت is an archaic past meaning “he slept” (ب + خفت). The sequence describes a violent act followed by sleep at that place.
segments.s115-g53: چو = temporal conjunction “when.” بشنید is 3rd-person past “(he/they) heard.” اولاد “the children/offspring” is the (grammatical) subject here; برگشت is “turned back/returned,” and زود is an adverb “soon/quickly.” Word order in poetry allows the verb and subject to be arranged for meter.
segments.s115-g54: برون آمد is the compound past meaning “came out / emerged.” از + درد دل = “from the pain/sorrow of (his) heart,” an idiomatic phrase for deep grief. همچو دود is a simile “like smoke,” completing the image: he (or they, depending on the referent) emerged from sorrow as if like smoke.
segments.s115-g55: که تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause (“so that / in order that”). بنگرد is a subjunctive/optative “may look / may behold.” کاو is an older/formal element here functioning as a relative/interrogative (contextually “who/what”), and چه مردست = “what sort of man he is” (چه = what, مردست = ‘is a man’/what kind of man he is). خود is emphatic “himself.” Altogether: “that he might look to see what sort of man he himself was.”
segments.s115-g56: ابا here is the verb ‘to refuse / be reluctant’ in past (he refused). او = him. ز بهر = poetic ordering meaning “for / because of.” چه = “what.” کردست is a perfect/past auxiliary-like form “has done” (he had done), and بد = “bad / evil.” The clause asks/notes that he was refused because of whatever bad thing he had done.
segments.s115-g81: تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun (subject). با این سپه is a prepositional phrase 'with/leading this army' placed after the subject; Persian often puts such adjuncts before the verb. پیش من is a locative phrase 'before me / in my presence' where پیش functions as a preposition; رانده‌ای is the perfective 2nd-person singular form (رانده + -ای) meaning 'you have driven' or 'you have led/driven away' and closes the clause.
segments.s115-g82: همی is a classical adverbial particle marking ongoing or emphatic speech here ('still/indeed'); گو is the verb 'say' in the present/imperative stem used idiomatically as 'you say/you claim'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', برگنبد is a locative noun 'on the dome / on the vault', and افشانده‌ای is the perfective 2nd-person singular 'you have scattered/strewn' (transitive; object is omitted but understood from context).
segments.s115-g83: نهنگ بلا is a poetic compound ('whale of calamity' = a monstrous calamity). برکشید is a past tense verb (3rd-person singular) meaning 'he drew/raised' and here is used with از نیام ('from the scabbard'); از + نیام is the source phrase 'out of/from the sheath'.
segments.s115-g84: بیاویخت is the past 3rd-person of آویختن 'to hang' (here 'he hung [it]/let it dangle' or 'he swung it out'). از پیش is a prepositional phrase 'from/in front of' or 'before', زین is 'the saddle', خم is 'the curve/bend' of the saddle, and خام is an adjectival epithet 'raw/untempered' modifying خم; the clause therefore describes how/where he hung or displayed the weapon.
segments.s115-g85: چو is the classical subordinating particle 'when' (not simile). شیر is the noun 'lion'. اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase where اندر is an old locative preposition 'into/within' and آمد is the past 'came' — together 'entered'. میان بره places شیر 'in the midst of the lambs' (میان = midst/among, بره = lamb).
segments.s115-g86: همه رزمگه شد is a nominal clause with شد 'became' (past 3sg) — 'the whole (place) became a battlefield' or 'the whole battlefield became ...'. ز introduces the cause/ingredient 'from/through', followed by the compound کشته خره where کشته ('the slain') governs خره (a poetic noun 'clod/heap/chunk'), yielding the image 'the whole battlefield became (one) heap/from the slain' (i.e., covered with bodies).
segments.s115-g87: به یک زخم is an ablative/agent phrase 'by/with one wound'. دو دو is a reduplicated numeral meaning 'two by two' (pairwise). سرافگند and خوار are predicative/adjectival terms following the numeral, 'ashamed' and 'lowly/despised' respectively — together describing the state of those who fell. The phrase thus means 'by one wound, two by two they were cast down ashamed and abased.'
segments.s115-g88: همی is the narrative/emphatic particle marking the statement; یافت is the past tense 'was found/found' (here impersonal or general 'there were found'). از تن is 'from the body' (literally) or 'from a man/person', and به یک تن چهار is a compressed quantitative phrase 'for one person, four' (i.e. 'from one person four were found' — a terse epic way to state the multiplicity of casualties). Word order is compact and emphatic: particle + verb + source phrase + quantity phrase.
segments.s115-g145: "وزان" is the poetic compound 'و + زان' = 'and/from that', marking the starting point; "روی بزگوش" is a noun phrase 'the goat-eared face/side' (روی = face/side + بزگوش adjective); "تا" here means 'as far as / to' and introduces the extent; "نرم پای" is a compound descriptive name 'soft-foot' (نرم = soft, پای = foot) functioning as the endpoint of the range.
segments.s115-g146: "چو" functions as a simile/introducer 'as' (here linking the following measure); "فرسنگ سیصد" is the quantified distance 'three hundred parasangs' (فرسنگ modified by the numeral); "کشیده" is a participle/adjective 'stretched/extended' describing the following noun; "سرای" (here a dwelling/house) is the head noun described by "کشیده" — together meaning 'a dwelling stretched (to) three hundred parasangs.'
segments.s115-g147: "ز" is the poetic preposition 'from'; it begins a range 'from X to Y'. "بزگوش" again describes the starting place as 'goat-eared'. "تا" marks the other end 'to/as far as'; "شاه مازندران" is the destination noun phrase 'the Shah (king) of Mazandaran' (شاه head noun + proper name as genitive).
segments.s115-g148: "رهی" here names a route/road (a poetic form related to ره) and is modified by the following adjective "زشت" 'ugly/foul'. The conjunction "و" links this phrase to the next noun phrase; "فرسنگهای" is the plural/collective 'parasangs (distances)' and "گران" is an adjective 'heavy/weighty' that qualifies those distances — together describing a harsh, onerous stretch of measured distance.
segments.s115-g149: "پراگنده" is an adjective/adverb 'scattered/widespread' placed before the noun phrase; "در پادشاهی" is the locative prepositional phrase 'in the kingdom'; "سوار" is the head noun 'riders/horsemen' — together: 'riders scattered through the kingdom.' Word order is adjective + locative phrase + noun for poetic emphasis.
segments.s115-g150: "همانا" is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. "که" introduces the substantive/content clause 'that ...' and "هستند" is the present-tense plural copula 'they are/there are'. "سیصدهزار" is the numeral 'three hundred thousand' as the predicate of existence — 'indeed there are three hundred thousand.'
segments.s115-g151: "ز" is the poetic 'from/of' used here to introduce a counted class. "پیلان" is the plural 'elephants' (here 'war-elephants'); "جنگی" is a postpositive adjective 'war/warrior' specifying these elephants as military. The numerals (هزار و دویست) follow to give the count 'one thousand and two hundred.'
segments.s115-g152: "کزیشان" is a fused relative/partitive 'of/from them' (کز + ایشان) referring back to the elephants; "به شهر اندرون" is a locative phrase 'into/within the city (interior)'; "جای نیست" is an existential negation 'there is no place/room' — literally, 'of them, for inside the city there is no place.'
segments.s115-g41: بدان here is an archaic locative 'to/at that' (built from the prefix ب- + an old form), followed by مرز 'border/frontier'. اولاد is a noun 'offspring/children' and بد پهلوان is a compound attributive: بد 'bad/evil' qualifies پهلوان 'champion/hero'. Word order is roughly locative phrase + subject + adjectival phrase describing that subject.
segments.s115-g42: یکی introduces a single individual 'a certain/one'. نامجوی is an adjective meaning 'name-seeking' (i.e. fame-seeking). دلیر و جوان are two coordinating adjectives 'brave and young', with و linking them; جوان here denotes a young man. The whole phrase apposes/identifies the particular offspring mentioned just before.
segments.s115-g43: بشد is classical/poetic past (or perfective) meaning 'he came/it occurred' and introduces the action; the subject دشتبان 'herdsman' follows the verb (inverted order). پیش او = 'before him/in his presence'. با خروش 'with a cry' is an instrumental/attendant circumstantial phrase describing how he came.
segments.s115-g44: پر از خون is an adjectival phrase 'full of blood' describing the herdsman (i.e. bloodied). به دستش گرفته is a participial/relative construction 'having taken / holding in his hand' where گرفته is a past participle 'taken/held' and ش is the enclitic 'his'; two گوش (literally 'two ears') is the object held. Word order places the participial phrase and object after the main clause.
segments.s115-g45: بدو گفت is the formula 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' as indirect object, گفت = 'said' in the past). مردی چو دیو سیاه is a simile: مردی 'a man' + چو 'like/as' + دیو سیاه 'a black demon' — i.e. 'a man like a black demon.'
segments.s115-g46: پلنگینه جوشن is a noun phrase describing his dress — پلنگینه 'leopard-skin (adjective or noun used adjectivally)' + جوشن 'cuirass/armor'. از آهن کلاه is another nominal phrase 'a helmet of iron' (از = 'of/from', آهن = 'iron', کلاه = 'hat/helmet'), the whole line listing his fearsome accoutrements. Punctuation (the semicolon) shows this is a single vivid description.
segments.s115-g47: همه دشت سرتاسر is an emphatic locative: 'the whole plain throughout'. آهرمنست is a predicative compound: آهرمن (Ahriman, the demon) + است 'is', i.e. 'is Ahriman' meaning 'is full of demonic presence/evil'. The clause is a parenthetical amplification of the previous description.
segments.s115-g48: وگر here means 'or if / or else', introducing an alternative image. اژدها خفته 'a dragon (is) sleeping' uses خفته (past participle) predicatively, and بر جوشنست puts the sleeping creature 'upon the cuirass/armor' (بر = 'on/upon', جوشنست = 'is on the armor'), giving a second terrifying possibility for the scene.
segments.s115-g73: آورد is a past-tense verb ('brought/put'); the subject is omitted (classical Persian often omits the pronoun). همه نیِزه و تیغ is a coordinated direct object ('all spears and swords') placed before the verb, so the clause reads literally 'brought all spears and swords.'
segments.s115-g74: سران را is the marked definite object ('the chiefs' or 'the heads of the chiefs') with را; the following phrase سر اندر کنار آورد repeats 'سر' (head) and uses اندر کنار ('beside/side by side') as a locative phrase before the verb آورد — literally 'he brought the heads into a side-by-side (position).' This duplication is a poetic/accumulative construction: object + specification + verb.
segments.s115-g75: به گوش تو‌گر is a conditional locative phrase: به گوش 'to the ear', تو‌گر 'if you' (archaic conditional particle). نام من is the subject/thing that might 'pass' and بگذرد (3rd-person verb form) is used as 'passes/reaches' — so the clause reads 'if my name passes to your ear.'
segments.s115-g76: A stacked series of nouns joined by و ('and') — دم و جان و خون و دل — ends with the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت on دل ('your heart'). The final verb بفسرد (third-person singular) expresses the effect on all those items ('will/shall grow cold' or 'will be chilled'); the b- prefix is the classical present/jussive marker. Grammatically the whole string is the consequence clause tied to the preceding conditional.
segments.s115-g77: نیامد is past negative ('did not come/reach'); به گوشت is the locative phrase ('to your hearing/ear') and به هر انجمن ('in/at every gathering') is an adverbial phrase giving the setting. Word order places the verb before the locatives (poetic inversion), yielding 'it did not reach your ear in any assembly.'
segments.s115-g78: کمند و کمان is a noun coordination ('lasso and bow'). گو here functions as a particle meaning 'called/said' or 'so-called' attached to the following name; پیلتن is a name/place. The phrase is a nominal tag: 'the lassos and bows called Piltan' or 'the talk “lasso and bow,” called Piltan.'
segments.s115-g79: هران (classical herān = 'whenever' / 'every time') introduces a general conditional clause. مام 'mother' is the subject; کاو is an archaic relative/connector here (functionally 'that/who' — the form is poetic/archaisms), چون تو 'like you' modifies پسر, and زاید is the verb 'gives birth' (3rd person). The whole reads 'whenever a mother bears a son like you.'
segments.s115-g80: کفن دوز is a compound noun 'kafan-dooz' (literally 'shroud-sewer' — one who sews shrouds). خوانیمش is a first-person-plural verb with object clitic ('we call/name him' or 'we read/call him'); ار is the conditional 'if', and مویه‌گر is the predicate noun 'a mourner/wailer.' Grammatically: 'we call him a shroud-sewer, if (he is) a wailer.'
segments.s115-g185: بپیچید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb (ب- prefix + پیچید) meaning 'twisted/entwined'; the clause has the object اولاد followed by the definite object-mark را, so word order is Verb + Object را + prepositional phrase (here the object appears before the preposition phrase for emphasis). بر درخت is a simple prepositional phrase meaning 'on/around the tree' with بر + noun.
segments.s115-g186: به خم کمندش درآویخت سخت: به خم + کمندش is a locative/goal phrase 'onto the bend of his lasso' (به = 'towards/into', خم = 'curve/bend', کمندش = کمند + possessive suffix -ش 'his lasso'); درآویخت is past 3rd-singular 'hung on / fastened to' (درآویختن) and here takes the previously marked object (the children) implicitly; سخت is an adverb 'firmly/strongly', modifying the verb and placed after it for emphasis.
segments.s115-g187: به زین اندر افگند گرز نیا: به زین اندر is a doubled locative 'into/within the saddle' (به = 'into/towards', زین = 'saddle', اندر = 'in/within'); افگند is past 3rd-singular 'threw/cast' and takes گرز ('mace') as its object. نیا follows and is likely a proper name or appellation placed after the clause (ambiguity in classical Persian—here read as the name 'Niyâ' associated with the following action).
segments.s115-g188: همی رفت یکدل پر از کیمیا: همی + رفت is a classical/perfective-imperfective idiom meaning 'kept going / went on' (همی indicates continued or ongoing action); یکدل is an adjective/adverbial 'single‑hearted, resolute'; پر از is the construction 'full of' (پر = 'full', followed by از = 'of/from'); کیمیا here is used figuratively as 'elixir/secret/transforming power'. Word order is subject/agent (Niyâ) implied from previous segment, then verb phrase with adjectival/adverbial complements.
segments.s115-g65: نشست is the 3rd-person past of نشستن used here = ‘he alighted/dismounted.’ از بر رخش is a prepositional phrase 'from off Rakhsh' (از = from; بر = on/upon; رخش = the steed Rakhsh). و رخشنده تیغ is a noun phrase ('and a shining/bright sword') where رخشنده is an epithet modifying تیغ and the verb that applies to it (drew) is postponed to the next segment.
segments.s115-g66: کشید is the 3rd-person past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw' and takes تیغ (from the previous segment) as its object: 'he drew (it).' و بیامد = 'and he came/advanced.' چو introduces a simile ('like') and غرنده میغ is the comparison 'a roaring blade' (غرنده modifying میغ). Word order places the simile after the verb phrase.
segments.s115-g67: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him.' گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (introducing the following interrogative clause). اولاد نام تو چیست is an interrogative clause 'what is the name of your offspring/child' with the possessor تو placed after نام (poetic inversion) and چی + ست forming the question 'what is...'.
segments.s115-g68: This is a sequence of short interrogative phrases: چه modifies مردی to ask 'what kind of man are you?' then the coordination و links further questions: 'and شاه و پناه تو کیست' = 'and who is your king and your protector?' کی + ست again forms the copular question 'who is...'. Word order is compact and typical of epic questioning.
segments.s115-g69: نبایست + کردن forms a prohibitive/modal phrase 'it ought not to be done / one should not' (here: 'one should not take'). برین is the poetic/orthographic form of بر این = 'on this' or 'this' (modifying the following noun). ره گذر = 'the way/pass' ('ره' = way, 'گذر' = passage), so together 'برین ره گذر' = 'this road/pass.'
segments.s115-g70: This nominal phrase characterizes the road: 'ره' (way) + نره (an epithet meaning 'manly/virile') + دیوان (here refers to supernatural fiends/demons in context — the given senses list doesn't include that meaning) + پرخاشخر 'quarrelsome/brawling.' Together: 'the way of the virile, quarrelsome devils.' The word order is attributive and compact, typical of epic description.
segments.s115-g71: چنین گفت رستم is an inverted order meaning 'thus spoke Rostam' (چنین = thus/so; گفت = 'said' as the past reporting verb; رستم is the speaker). که introduces the quoted content; نام من «ابر!» is a compact self-identification 'my name (is) “Abr!”' with the copula omitted as is common in direct speech.
segments.s115-g72: The parenthetical clause begins with اگر 'if' and repeats ابر (the word/name just given). باشد here is a subjunctive/optative form 'be' (used concessively: 'if it be [a cloud]'); به زور هژبر is an instrumental phrase 'by the force/might of Hezbar' (به = by; زور = force/power; هژبر = a proper name).
segments.s115-g17: برون کرد (literally 'he made out') is a past-tense verb phrase meaning 'he drew out' or 'he brought forth' (verb پیش‌وند برون + کرد as past). The direct object follows the verb: ببر بیان is a noun phrase in apposition — ببر 'the tiger' and بیان here functions as a proper name (Bayān) identifying that tiger. از برش is a prepositional phrase 'from his side/waist' (از + برش) marking the source.
segments.s115-g18: به introduces direction/target 'into/onto' (here 'into/onto the garment/inside'); خوی اندرون 'the inner part/inside of it' (خوی used here as 'disposition/inner part' referring back to the animal or its pelt) specifies the locus. غرقه (participle/adjective 'immersed/soaked') modifies مغفرش 'its covering/lining' (مغفر + enclitic ش 'its'); بد qualifies مغفرش as 'foul/dirty/ill' — overall: 'its inner part soaked its foul lining'.
segments.s115-g19: بگسترد is the past form 'he spread (out)' (بـ- prefix + گستردن). هر دو 'both' is the quantified object, and بر آفتاب 'on/under the sun' (بر + آفتاب) indicates where he spread them — idiomatically 'out in the sun/into sunlight'. Word order places the verb before the object, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s115-g20: به خواب و به آسایش 'to sleep and to repose' uses به as a directional/goal marker for abstract states. آمد شتاب literally 'haste came' — here شتاب functions adverbially/nominally indicating that sleep and comfort arrived quickly; آمد is simple past 'came'. The clause means 'sleep and comfort came quickly.'
segments.s115-g21: لگام از سر رخش برداشت is a straightforward verbal clause: لگام 'bridle' (direct object), از سر رخش 'from over Rakhsh's head' (prepositional source), برداشت past of برداشتن 'he removed/took off'. خوار immediately following functions as a descriptive epithet applied to the animal ('the lowly/wretched one'); here it characterizes the released beast.
segments.s115-g22: رها کرد 'he released/let loose' (رها + کرد). بر خوید is a verbal-nominal construction where خوید (archaic infinitive 'to eat/graze') indicates purpose/result — 'to graze' or 'for grazing'. در کشتزار 'in the planted field' gives the location. Together: 'he let it go to graze in the field.'
segments.s115-g23: بپوشید 'he covered himself/wrapped up' is past of پوشیدن; چون خشک شد خود is a temporal clause 'when he himself became dry' (چون here = 'when/since' marking the time, خشک شد = 'became dry', خود emphasizing 'himself'). The conjunction و links this clause to what follows.
segments.s115-g24: گیاکرد بستر is a compound expression 'made a bed of vegetation/grass' (گیاکرد literally 'grass-made' used as a verb: he 'made (a bed) of plants'). بسان هژبر is a simile 'like Hezhbar' (هژبر a proper name or epithet). Grammatically, the clause gives the second action parallel to the first: while he wrapped himself, the (tiger/animal) prepared a grassy bed in the manner of Hezhbar.
segments.s115-g153: نتابی is a negative present/near-future form (ن + تابى) meaning “you do not/will not shine”; the verb precedes the explicit pronoun تو (verb–subject order, common in epic style). تنها modifies تو (“you alone”). وگر introduces a conditional (“and if”), and ز + آهنی marks material/source (“if you are of iron”); ز here functions as the poetic preposition “from/of” specifying constitution.
segments.s115-g154: بسایدت is a 3rd-person singular verb base (بساید) with the enclitic object -ت “you” (literally “it/that will suffice you”); here it carries the sense ‘will suffice / will be enough (to wear you down)’. Syntactically the verb comes before its logical subject in this short clause; سوهان آهرمنی (file + adjective) is the agent/subject implied to do the wearing.
segments.s115-g155: چنان modifies لشکری and together they form ‘such a host/army’ (چنان = “such”). با introduces the instruments/means (‘with’): سلیح ‘weapons’ and درم ‘coin’ (درم = coin/dirham). Word order places the descriptive material after the noun in a compact, poetic phrase.
segments.s115-g156: نبینی is negative 2nd-person present/near-future (‘you do not / will not see’). ازیشان = از + ایشان “from/out of them / among them,” marking the partitive group. یکی را is ‘a single one’ with را as the definite/object marker used here to topicalize the one person; دژم is a predicate adjective ‘sullen/grim’ describing that one. The clause means you will not find even one of them who is sullen.
segments.s115-g157: بخندید is a past 3rd-person form ‘he laughed’ (here with the nuance ‘laughed at’ because of what follows). In epic word order the verb comes before the subject رستم. ز + گفتار اوی expresses cause/reason (‘because of his speech’), with ز used as ‘because of’ and اوی = ‘his/that of him’.
segments.s115-g158: بدو گفت is the common inversion ‘to him (bedo) he said’ (indirect object precedes the verb). اگر با منی is a conditional clause: اگر = ‘if’; با منی = ‘you are with me’ where ی is the clitic copula/ending marking the 2nd‑person state. راه جوی is a terse imperative/hortative phrase ‘seek the way’ (جوی from ‘جستن’ meaning ‘search/seek’).
segments.s115-g159: ببینی is the 2nd-person present/future form ‘you will see’ (or imperative/optative in some contexts). کزین is the contracted prepositional phrase (که + از + این) meaning ‘from this’ or ‘of this’. یک تن پیلتن is a noun phrase ‘one person of Piltan’ (تن = ‘person’ here), with the place name پیلتن qualifying the person.
segments.s115-g160: چه آید is an interrogative/indefinite clause ‘what will come’ or ‘what will result’; چه is the question/indefinite word and آید is the present/future ‘comes/will come’. بران = بر + آن ‘to/upon that’, and نامدار انجمن is the noun phrase ‘renowned assembly’ (adjective + noun) indicating the destination/context for whatever will happen.
segments.s115-g89: سران را is the definite direct object (سران + object marker را). The prepositional phrase ز زخمش ('by/from his wound') indicates the means or cause, followed by the destination phrase به خاک ('to the ground/onto the soil'). آوردید is an archaic/past plural verb form meaning 'they brought' (verb at end, typical SOV order).
segments.s115-g90: سر سرکشان is a noun + modifier construction: 'heads of the rebellious' (سر = head; سرکشان = the rebellious ones). زیر پی is a locative phrase 'under the foot/underfoot' (پی here = foot/track). گسترید is a past/plural verb 'they spread/laid' placed at the end to complete the clause.
segments.s115-g91: در و دشت ('the court/town and the plain') is the compound subject. شد پر ('became full') uses شد as a linking/past verb and پر as the predicative adjective 'full (of)'. ز گرد سوار ('with/from the dust of horsemen') is a genitive/means phrase explaining what filled them (گرد = dust, سوار = riders).
segments.s115-g92: پراگنده گشتند is a verbal phrase 'they became scattered' or 'they scattered' (گشتند = past plural 'became/turned'). بر کوه و غار is a locative complement 'upon/onto mountain and cave', showing where they dispersed. The plural subject is understood from the prior clause (the riders).
segments.s115-g93: همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle that precedes the verb. گشت here means 'turned / moved / acted' (past action) and pairs with the subject رستم. چو پیل دژم is a simile 'like a grim elephant' (چو = 'like/as', دژم = 'grim/sullen'), placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s115-g94: کمندی ('a lasso/snare') is the noun; به بازو ('on/around the arm') uses به in the sense 'towards/onto' specifying location. درون شصت خم ('within sixty bends') is a locative/quantifying phrase describing how the lasso lay — many coils — and خم here means 'curve/bend'. The clause is descriptive and the verb is omitted (typical in condensed poetic description).
segments.s115-g95: به اولاد ('toward the sons/men') is a prepositional phrase marking the indirect object or the group approached. چون here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic usage) introducing the subordinate clause. رخش is the subject (the horse's name) and نزدیک شد is the verb phrase 'drew near' (شد = past).
segments.s115-g96: به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/in the manner of (his) action' (به here = 'by/‑ly'). شب روز تاریک شد is a poetic inversion listing both 'night' and 'day' and concluding with تاریک شد 'became dark' — a hyperbolic way to say his deeds made the world dark (i.e., terrible fighting). شد is the past linking verb.
segments.s115-g113: بدو (به + او) is a poetic clitic meaning "to him/unto him," placed before the verb; گفت here is the past 3rd-person of گفتن "(he) said," introducing reported speech. اولاد دل is a genitive phrase "offspring/children of the heart" with را marking it as the definite/direct object, and ز خشم is the poetic preposition "from/away from anger" modifying what should be driven away.
segments.s115-g114: بپرداز is an imperative form meaning "cast away/drive off" or "attend to and remove" (here an order addressed to the addressee). و is the coordinating "and." بگشای is an imperative "open" (second verb in the same command), یکباره "all at once/at once," and چشم is the direct object "eye(s)" (open your eyes).
segments.s115-g115: تن من is the noun + possessive pronoun "my body." مپرداز is a negative imperative/jussive meaning "do not cast away/put aside" (prohibiting harm to the speaker). خیره ز جان is a predicative phrase: خیره "astonished/dazed" (or "gazing/astounded") governed by ز "from/through" + جان "soul, life" to mean "dazed in spirit."
segments.s115-g116: بیابی is a present/subjunctive or modal form meaning "you will find/you shall obtain" (second-person sense directed at the addressee). ز من "from me," هرچ "whatever," خواهی is the second-person present of خواستن "to want" ("you want"); همان functions as a resumptive demonstrative "that same (thing)." Together: "you will obtain from me whatever you want."
segments.s115-g117: ترا here stands for the dative/accusative "to you" (historically ت + را); it marks the addressee. خانه بید is a noun phrase "the house of the willow" (بید = willow) and دیو سپید is a nominal apposition "the White Demon" (دیو + adjective سپید). The whole phrase is the object/recipient of the verb in the next segment (what will be shown to you).
segments.s115-g118: نمایم is a first-person verb form "I will show / I show" (here declarative/future sense). من is the subject "I." این را "this" with را as object marker points forward to the clause introduced by که; که introduces a relative/clausal complement meaning "which/that." دادی is second-person past "you gave" and نوید is "promise/pledge/omen" — together: "this that you gave as a promise" (i.e., what you promised).
segments.s115-g119: به جایی "to/into the place" where که بستست is a relative clause introduced by که "where/that"; بستست is a past form meaning "(he) closed/was closed/was shut up" (here passive/figurative: Kavus being confined). کاووس شاه is the noun + title "Kavus the king." The clause gives the destination/place referenced in the next segment.
segments.s115-g120: بگویم is a verb meaning "I will tell / I shall say" (first-person, here prospective); ترا is the addressee-marker "to you" (t + را). یک به یک is an idiom "one by one; individually." شهر و راه "cities and roads" are the direct objects (what will be recounted). The whole segment: "I will tell you, one by one, the towns and roads."
segments.s115-g105: به + جایی = 'to a place' (preposition + noun). که introduces a relative clause. بستست is an archaic/past perfect‑style 3rd‑singular form 'has shut / shut up' and appears before the subject (کاووس) by poetic inversion. کی here is the interrogative/relative 'who' that links forward to the next clause.
segments.s115-g106: کسی is 'someone' (the indefinite person referred to by کی). کاین = 'that/these' (demonstrative + relative), بدیها is the plural 'evils/harms', and فگندست is an archaic past 'threw' (3rd sg.). پی is a postposition 'after / following' (i.e. 'after him'). The clause gives who did the throwing; word order and some verb forms are archaic/poetic.
segments.s115-g107: نمایی is an imperative/2nd‑person verbal idea 'show / make appear' (poetic present form). و links clauses. پیدا کنی is a light‑verb construction: پیدا (visible / apparent) + کنی (2nd‑person present/subjunctive of کردن) meaning 'you make apparent / you discover'. راستی is the direct object 'the truth.'
segments.s115-g108: نیاری here is problematic in the transmitted lemma: it does not fit the listed noun senses ('lover/companion'); in context it functions as a negative/absence or agentive idea ('no need / no helper' or 'you do not bring') — the exact morphological source is archaic or corrupt. به کار اندرون = 'for/to the work (affairs) of the inner [quarters]'. کاستی is a postpositive noun 'deficiency/shortcoming'. The whole phrase means roughly 'you will not need a helper for the inner affairs' shortcomings' (note textual/lexical uncertainty about نیاری).
segments.s115-g109: من is the 1st‑person subject 'I'. The sequence این تخت و این تاج و گرز گران is a coordinated list of direct objects with repeated demonstrative این for emphasis; گران is a postpositive adjective 'heavy / precious.'
segments.s115-g110: بگردانم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive/future of گرداندن meaning 'I will turn / cause to be turned / take away' (here transitive: 'I will take away'). از introduces the agent/source 'from', and شاه مازندران is the prepositional object 'the king of Mazandaran.' The verb precedes the prepositional phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s115-g111: تو باشی is a 2nd‑person jussive/present 'you shall be' or 'you should be' (poetic). بر + ین (here orthographic/demonstrative element) with بوم yields برین بوم 'on/over this land'. و بر شهریار adds 'and over the sovereign/king' — together meaning 'you shall rule this land and be its monarch.'
segments.s115-g112: ار is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small adverbial/particle meaning roughly 'in this way' or 'so' (textual/archaic). کژی is the noun 'crookedness / wrongness'. نیاری again is lexically uncertain in the provided lemma; context strongly suggests a verbal sense 'you do not bring / introduce' or a negated necessity 'you will not need' — here best read as 'you do not bring/apply'. بکار is a compound adverb/verb complement 'into use / into operation' (i.e. 'apply'). The clause therefore means 'if you do not apply crookedness thus.'
segments.s115-g97: بیفگند is a past-tense, 3rd-person verb meaning 'he threw/ cast' (classical/poetic form with the b- prefix). The subject رستم follows the verb (typical poetic word order V S O). کمند دراز is the direct object: کمند 'lasso' with the adjective دراز 'long' placed after the noun, the normal Persian adjective order.
segments.s115-g98: به خم اندر آمد is a clause 'into/ toward the bend came' where آمد is the verb 'came' and the prepositional frame به خم اندر indicates movement 'into the loop/bend' (اندر = in/within). سر سرفراز is the noun phrase 'the proud/uplifted head' (سر 'head' + postpositive adjective سرفراز 'proud, victorious'); the phrase functions as the subject that has 'come' into the loop.
segments.s115-g99: از اسپ اندر آمد literally 'came down from the steed' (از = from/out of, اسپ = horse/steed, اندر آمد = came in/ alighted — a classical phrasing for dismounting). دو دستش ببست is 'he bound his two hands' where دو دست 'two hands' is followed by the possessive clitic ش 'his', and ببست is the 3rd-person past 'he bound/tied' (here meaning he tied/secured his hands).
segments.s115-g100: بپیش اندر افگند is 'he cast/threw it forward' (بپیش = forward/toward the front, اندر افگند = threw in/into — classical order). The object of 'threw' is omitted in the line but contextually refers to what he cast. و خود برنشست links the two actions with و 'and'; خود is emphatic 'himself' and برنشست is a past tense compound meaning 'sat upon / took his seat', so 'and he himself sat down.'
segments.s115-g101: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' where بدو = 'to him' (dative clitic form) precedes گفت 'said' — a common order in classical Persian indirect speech. اگر راست‌گویی سخن is a conditional nominal clause 'if (your) truth-speaking / if the speech is truthful' where راست‌گویی (truthfulness / truth-speaking) modifies or stands with سخن 'speech/words'; the whole phrase marks the condition for what follows.
segments.s115-g102: ز کژی نه سر یابم از تو نه بن is a negative result clause. ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/of'; کژی = 'crookedness/wrongdoing'; نه ... یابم = 'I will not find' (یابم here = 1st-person present/subjunctive used to express 'I will/should find' in classical style, negated by نه). از تو نه بن literally 'from you nor root' places 'you' as the locus: 'I will find in you neither head (سر) nor root/base (بن) of crookedness' — i.e., 'I will find no trace of wrongdoing in you.'
segments.s115-g103: نمایی here functions as an imperative/subjunctive 'show (me)' or a verbal noun meaning 'showing' used as a request; مرا is the object 'me' (broken into morphemes in the word list), and جای دیو سپید is the noun phrase 'the place of the white demon' (جای = place, دیو = demon, سپید = white) — direct object of the request.
segments.s115-g104: همان جای is a demonstrative-modified noun phrase 'the same/that very place' (همان = that/the same, جای = place). پولاد غندی و بید is a coordinated apposition or list of names/terms specifying the place (پولاد, غندی, بید) joined by و 'and' — in context these function as identifiers of the exact spot; the adjective order and conjunction are standard.
segments.s115-g57: همی is a continuous/emphatic particle here ('kept on', 'were/continued to'); گشت is past/impersonal 'went/roamed' (here 'were roaming'); اولاد is the noun 'sons/offspring' and follows the verb phrase (verb-first order is common in epic Persian); در مرغزار is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the meadow'.
segments.s115-g58: This short clause gives the participants and purpose: نامداران is an adjectival/noun plural 'the famed/renowned (ones)'; ز بهر شکار is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of the hunt' (ز + بهر = poetic 'for'); ابا here stands between the verb clause and the appositive 'نامداران' — in this line it functions as an archaic/poetic connector attaching 'اولاد' to 'نامداران' (i.e. 'the offspring of the famed').
segments.s115-g59: چو = 'when'; از دشتبان is an agent/instrumental phrase 'from the herdsman' (i.e. 'as told by / when the herdsman'); این شگفتی is the object phrase 'this wonder/strange thing'; شنید is the past tense 'heard' (simple past — here 'heard of' or 'was told'). Word order puts the object phrase before the verb, a normal option in Persian poetry.
segments.s115-g60: به نخچیر گه = 'at/into the hunting-place' (به + نخچیرگه, a locative); بر پی شیر = 'on the track/footprint of the lion' (بر = 'on/upon', پی here = 'track/trace'); دید is simple past 'he saw'. The phrase order is locative → target → verb, typical in narrative lines.
segments.s115-g61: عنان را is the definite object with the postposition را ('the reins'); بتابید is the past form of تابیدن used causatively/lexically here 'turned/whirled' (i.e. 'set the reins turning' = 'turned the reins'); با سرکشان is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with (his) ...' — سرکشان is a poetic plural describing the companions/steeds (an epithet; the exact lexical sense is archaising), so the whole means 'he turned the reins with his spirited ones/horsemen'.
segments.s115-g62: بدان سو = 'to/toward that side' (بدان is the fused prepositional/demonstrative 'to that'); که بود = 'which was' (relative clause introducer + past 'was'); از تهمتن نشان = 'a sign of/from Tahmatan' (از = 'from/of', تهمتن = the hero's name/epithet, نشان = 'sign, token'). The relative clause identifies the direction toward which he turned.
segments.s115-g63: چو آمد = 'when (he) came/entered' (چو = when, آمد = came); به تنگ اندرون = 'into the narrow inner space' (به + adjective + noun giving the place); جنگجوی follows as a nominal apposition/subject 'the warrior' — in poetic word order the descriptive noun can come after the locative verbal phrase.
segments.s115-g64: تهمتن is the subject (the hero's epithet); سوی رخش = 'toward Rakhsh' (سوی = toward, رخش = the steed Rakhsh); بنهاد روی is a verbal idiom 'he set/laid (his) face' = 'he turned his face' (بنهاد from نهادن 'to put/place', روی = 'face' as the thing turned). The verb comes after its object in this poetic line.
segments.s115-g177: تهمتن is the proper name (subject). به + اولاد marks the indirect object/recipient ('to the youths/sons'). گفت is the narrative past 'said' introducing reported speech; آن کجاست is an embedded question/phrase with کجا + ست (copula) meaning 'where is (that)'; the demonstrative آن precedes the question for emphasis — a slightly archaic/poetic word order.
segments.s115-g178: که introduces the subordinate clause ('where/that'). آتش برآمد is a past narrative construction ('fire rose/erupted'); همی is an older adverbial particle meaning 'continually/kept' (poetic) and appears after the verb here (word order freer in epic Persian). چپ و راست is a fixed coordinate phrase 'left and right' meaning 'on all sides'; و is the simple conjunction.
segments.s115-g179: در شهر مازندران است is a locative clause 'it is in the city of Mazandaran' (در + noun phrase + copula است). گفت follows the clause as the reporting verb ('he said'); this postposed placement is common in narrative (clause + گفت).
segments.s115-g180: که introduces reported content ('that'). از شب 'from/during the night' gives the time frame. دو بهره is 'two shares/portions' (here 'two watches/parts of the night'). نیارند خفت forms a negative predicate meaning roughly 'they do not obtain/take sleep' — نیارند is an older/poetic verbal form here with third-person plural sense and خفت (sleep) as its object/complement.
segments.s115-g181: بدان جایگه is a fused prepositional phrase (ب + آن = 'to/in that') + جایگه 'place' meaning 'in/at that place'. باشد here functions as an optical/future-like copula 'there will be' (classical/poetic use). ارژنگ دیو is the name with the noun 'demon' in apposition: 'Arzhang the demon.'
segments.s115-g182: که acts as a relative pronoun 'who/which' linking to 'ارژنگ دیو'. هزمان is an adverb 'simultaneously/at the same time'. برآید is a present/future-oriented or optative form 'will rise/shall spring forth'; خروش و غریو are the coordinated nouns 'roar and clamour' that are raised by the verb.
segments.s115-g183: بخفت is the past narrative verb 'he slept/was sleeping'. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb (epic word order: verb then adverbial). رستم جنگجوی is the subject in apposition 'Rostam the warrior' following the temporal phrase.
segments.s115-g184: چو is a simile/temporal conjunction 'as/when'. خورشید تابنده is a noun + adjective 'the shining sun' (adjective after noun). بنمود is an older past form 'showed' (بنمود = نمود with an augment), and روی is the direct object 'face' — together 'he showed (his) face like the shining sun.'
segments.s115-g129: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/among' introducing a partitive group. دیوان (here used poetically as the plural of دیو, 'divs' or demons) is the noun being counted; جنگی is an adjectival/nominal qualifier 'warlike' or 'warriors'. ده و دو هزار is a classical numeral phrase meaning '(ten and two) thousand' = twelve thousand (the small-number phrase precedes هزار to give the multiplier).
segments.s115-g130: به شب is an adverbial phrase 'by night/at night' (به used adverbially). پاسبانند is a present/stative verb 'they are guards/they guard' (ending -ند marks third-person plural). بر introduces the locus 'on/upon', and چاهسار is the place 'well-field / area of wells'.
segments.s115-g131: چو here introduces an example or named individual ('such as / there was'), followed by a proper name: پولاد غندی (Polād Ghandī) — پولاد is a personal name derived from 'steel'. سپهدار is a title 'commander'. اوی is a third‑person pronoun 'he'. The whole phrase means 'there was Polād Ghandī, a commander.'
segments.s115-g132: چو again introduces the next named figures. بیدست and سنجه appear as proper names here (though lexically bī-dast/sanj(e) have other meanings); they are joined by و 'and'. نگهدار is the agent noun 'guardian/keeper'. اوی in this position functions possessively/pronominally ('his' or 'of him'), indicating these were his keepers.
segments.s115-g133: یکی = 'one' (a certain one). کوه یابی is a poetic descriptor: کوه 'mountain' plus a verbal/derivative element یابی, giving the sense 'mountain‑sized' or 'mountain‑like' (a lofty/huge one) in context. مر 'to me' is the dative/experiencer pronoun, او را is the topicalized object 'him', and به تن = 'on the body'. Together: 'one, mountain‑sized to me, on his body...'.
segments.s115-g134: A location list introduced by بر 'on/upon': بر و کتف و یالش = 'on (the) shoulder and (the) flank and (his) mane'. بود is the past copula 'was' (here marking existence/possession in past narrative), and ده رسن = 'ten reins' (ده = ten modifying رسن 'reins/ropes').
segments.s115-g135: ترا is the object 'to you' composed of the 2nd‑person suffix ت plus the object marker را (here written as separate items). با introduces accompaniment 'with'; چنین is 'such/so', followed by a list of instruments/attributes یال و دست و عنان 'mane and hand and bridle'. The whole phrase marks what you would be given/put with.
segments.s115-g136: گذارنده is the active participial agent 'one who puts/places/gives' (often used as 'the giver/installer'). It is followed by a coordination of weapons: گرز و تیغ و سنان 'mace and sword and spear' — the direct objects of the placing/giving action. The clause is elliptical: 'the one who would equip you ... (would) place mace, sword and spear.'
segments.s115-g161: به is the instrumental/prepositional particle 'by'; نیروی یزدان is a genitive construction 'the power/strength of God' (نیروی = 'power/strength' with the genitive relation to یزدان). پیروزگر is an epithet meaning 'victorious' that qualifies the divine power; word order is preposition + noun (with implicit genitive) + epithet in poetic economy.
segments.s115-g162: The second phrase repeats the instrumental به ('by') to list means: بخت ('fate/fortune'), and then 'by the sword (شمشیر) sharp (تیز)' where تیز postmodifies شمشیر, followed by و هنر ('and skill'). The repeated به marks each item as an instrument or cause.
segments.s115-g163: چو + بینند is a temporal subordinate clause 'when they see' (چو = 'when'); تاو بر و یال من literally 'the sheen/gleam upon (بر) my back and mane' — note the possessive 'من' follows the nouns in this poetic ordering instead of using an ezāfe.
segments.s115-g164: به جنگ اندرون = 'into/within the battle' (به + جنگ + اندرون). زخم گوپال من places زخم ('wound') before the name گوپال with the possessive من after it ('my Gopala' or 'my Gopala's wound'); there is no explicit verb in this line — the action 'they wound/inflict' is implied by the context, a common elliptical feature in poetry.
segments.s115-g165: به درد پی و پوستشان از نهیب = 'to/for the pain of their feet and their skins from the lash/cry' — به درد marks the affliction, پی here means 'feet' (poetic use), پوستشان 'their hide/skin' (possessive suffix -شان). از نهیب indicates the source ('from the striking shout/ lash'); the noun order and prepositions reflect classical/poetic usage.
segments.s115-g166: عنان را ندانند باز از رکیب = 'they do not know how to free/release the reins from the rival.' عنان 'reins' is the definite object marked by را; ندانند is negative present 'they do not know' (or 'they are unable'); باز here has the sense 'to free/release' (poetic usage), and از رکیب 'from the opponent/rival' names the source/holder of the reins.
segments.s115-g167: ازان سو کجا هست کاووس کی = a compressed interrogative/relative clause: 'From that side/there—where is Kavus, who...?' ازان سو sets the location 'from that side'; کجا هست is the question 'where is'; کاووس is the proper name; کی functions here like 'who' (relative/interrogative) linking to the following request.
segments.s115-g168: مرا راه بنمای و بردار پی = an imperative plea: 'show me the way (مرا = me + را object marking) and take up/take on the pursuit (بردار پی).' بنمای is an imperative 'show!' (بنما), and بردار is an imperative 'take up' or 'lift' applied to پی ('pursuit/track'), so the speaker asks Kavus to show the road and either resume or remove the pursuit depending on context.
segments.s115-g137: چنین is a demonstrative meaning “such.” برز و بالا are two coordinate adjectives/nouns ('exalted' / 'lofty') linked by و. این کار is the direct object 'this deed/act' and کرد is the simple past of کردن ('did/made'), so the whole clause reads literally 'did this such exalted and lofty deed.'
segments.s115-g138: نه خوب است is a negated predicative clause 'it is not good.' The following phrase با دیو جستن نبرد uses جستن in its infinitival sense ('to attempt/seek') combined with the noun نبرد ('battle/fighting')—an older/poetic construction meaning 'to take up battle with a demon.' با marks the opponent 'with/against.'
segments.s115-g139: کزو is a fused relative 'from which' (کِ + ز او), and بگذری is the present/subjunctive/2nd-person form 'you pass' used in the relative clause. سنگلاخست is adjectival+copula ('is rocky' — سنگلاخ + -ست), and و دشت adds 'and (a) plain,' listing the terrain characteristics.
segments.s115-g140: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'which/that.' آهو is the subject 'the gazelle.' بران is an adverbial/location 'on/along that [path].' ره is 'way/road.' نیارد in the provided lexicon does not match the expected verbal sense here (it functions in the text like a negative/finding verb), so its proper sense is not available in the given senses. گذشت is the past verb 'passed' (or 'lies/presents itself' in poetic usage).
segments.s115-g141: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' ز (زه/زو) is the poetic preposition 'from/of (it).' بگذری again is 'you pass' (present/subjunctive). رود is the noun 'river.' آبست is an older/fused form meaning 'is water / filled with water' (here describing the river as full of water). پیش is an adverb 'before/in front (of you).' The clause is a verbless or nominal description typical of epic style: 'when you pass it, a river lies before you.'
segments.s115-g142: که introduces a relative clause 'whose/that.' پهنای او is a genitive construction 'its width' (پهنای = 'width of' + او = 'its'). بر here means 'over/above' (i.e., 'more than'), followed by the measure دو فرسنگ (two farsangs) and بیش 'more.' So the phrase states the river's breadth exceeds two farsangs.
segments.s115-g143: کنارنگ is an adverbial/adjectival phrase 'by/at the bank' (beside). دیوی is 'a demon' (دیو with the adjectival/genitive ending -ی). نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/keeper.' اوی is an archaic third‑person possessive/pronoun 'his/its' (here 'its keeper'). The clause literally: 'By the bank a demon is its keeper.'
segments.s115-g144: همه 'all' modifies the following noun phrase. نره qualifies دیوان as 'male/virile' (i.e., 'male demons' or emphatically 'brave demons'). In the manuscript دیوان functions as the plural of دیو ('demons'), but that sense is not in the provided list, so its sense is marked unavailable. به فرمان اوی is a prepositional phrase 'under/by his command' (به + فرمان = 'by command,' اوی = 'his/its'), indicating the demons act at his order.
segments.s115-g33: چرا introduces a direct question ('why'). اسپ (horse) is the direct object/topic placed before the prepositional phrase بر خوید. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and خوید is an archaic infinitive of خوردن meaning 'to eat, to graze'. بگذاشتی is 2nd‑person singular past (you + گذاشتی) with the b‑prefix common in classical Persian here meaning 'you let/placed/allowed'; it governs the preceding object phrase.
segments.s115-g34: بر رنج is a prepositional phrase 'upon (the) hardship', with نابرده (literally 'not‑borne/untasted') modifying رنج to mean 'not previously endured/untested'. برداشتی is 2nd‑person singular past of برداشتن 'you took (upon yourself)' so the clause reads 'you took upon yourself untried hardship'.
segments.s115-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'; گفتار او = 'his speech/words' (possessive order). تیز شد is the compound verb 'became sharp/keen' (شد = 'became'). مرد هوش is an inversion (in classical word‑order the noun phrase 'هوش مرد' = 'the man's wits/intelligence' is placed after the predicate).
segments.s115-g36: بجست is past 'he leapt/sprung' (an intransitive perfective). و simply joins the actions. گرفتش is گرفت + enclitic ‑ش 'him' meaning 'he seized him'; the object clitic attaches to the verb. یکایک 'one by one' quantifies دو گوش 'the two ears' and follows the verb as its object phrase.
segments.s115-g37: This clause describes violent action: بیفشرد is an archaic past form related to فشردن ('to press/squeeze') and here functions as a simple past verb; برکند is past of برکندن 'to pull up/out' (often 'uproot' or 'pluck out'). هر دو ز بن = 'both from the root/base' (ز بن = 'out of the root/from the base'), so together the clause means he crushed/squeezed and pulled both out from their roots. (Note: the supplied senses list did not include a straightforward positive sense 'pressed/squeezed', so that single entry is marked below as -1.)
segments.s115-g38: نگفت here is the negative past form 'he did not say' (نـ + گفت); the order is somewhat marked compared with modern colloquial 'با او سخن نگفت' but means 'he spoke no words with him'. از بد و نیک = 'of bad and good' (i.e. neither ill nor well was said), با او سخن = 'a speech/conversation with him'. The clause as a whole: 'he said nothing to him, neither good nor bad.' (The provided sense entries for نگفت were nouns/labels rather than the negative past verb sense, so نگفت is assigned -1 below.)
segments.s115-g39: سبک modifies دشتبان and here means 'light/agile/nimble' (an attributive adjective before the noun). گوش را uses the definite object marker را (so 'his ear/the ear') and برگرفت is past 'took up/raised' (here idiomatically 'pricked up his ears', i.e. paid attention).
segments.s115-g40: This clause gives a short result: غریوان is treated here as a proper name or label of a person (in other contexts the word can mean 'neckline/bosom'), و links it to the predicate. مانده is the past participle/adjective 'left/remaining' used predicatively ('remained'), ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/at', رستم is the proper name, and شگفت is an adjective/noun meaning 'astonished/wonder' used predicatively; together: 'Gharivān remained astonished at Rostam.'
segments.s115-g121: از ایدر (literally 'from here') is a prepositional phrase marking the starting point. به نزدیک کاووس is another prepositional phrase ('to/near Kavus') where نزدیک functions as 'vicinity' and کاووس is the name; کی is an archaic/poetic relative pronoun ('who/which') attached to کاووس and points forward to the action in the next segment.
segments.s115-g122: صد (hundred) quantitatively modifies فرسنگ (parasangs) though word order is inverted for poetic effect; افگنده is a past participle/verb meaning 'laid/cast out' describing the distance, and بخشیده is another participle 'granted/given' used here as a verbal modifier (poetic intensifier). پی follows فرسنگ to indicate the contiguous distance ('in succession' / 'following' — idiomatically giving the sense 'a hundred parasangs long').
segments.s115-g123: وزانجا is the conjunction + locative 'and from there' (و + ازانجا); سوی is the directional preposition 'toward(s)'; دیو is the destination 'the demon'; فرسنگ here begins the measure phrase 'parasangs' with the numeral صد implied nearby (poetic splitting of the phrase). The whole chunk sets up the direction and measured distance toward the demon.
segments.s115-g124: بیاید is a poetic present/impersonal verb 'comes/extends' (third person); یکی is the numeral 'one/a single' modifying راه 'road'; دشوار و بد are postpositive adjectives (typical in poetry) describing the road as 'difficult and bad/terrible'. Word order places the verb before its noun for stylistic inversion.
segments.s115-g125: میان is the preposition 'between'; دو صد is the numeral phrase 'two hundred' (numeral before the counted unit); چاهساری is a compound noun 'well-field(s)' and شگفت is a postpositive adjective 'wondrous/strange' modifying it—typical adjective-after-noun order in poetry.
segments.s115-g126: به here functions instrumentally ('by/with') so به پیمایش = 'by surveying/measurement'; اندازه is the noun 'measure/extent' (the thing attempted); نتوان is the negative potential 'cannot' (poetic present of نتوانستن) and گرفت is the infinitive/past-stem 'to take/obtain' — together نتوان گرفت = 'cannot be obtained/determined'.
segments.s115-g127: میان دو کوهست is a poetic contraction meaning 'is between two mountains' (کوهست here corresponds to 'کوه است' = 'mountains/are mountains'); این هول جای is the noun phrase 'this fearful place' with این as demonstrative, هول 'terror/fearful', and جای 'place'. The clause states location with an inverted/orderly poetic structure.
segments.s115-g128: نپرید is the negated past of پریدن 'did not fly' (negative prefix ن- plus past form); بر آسمان marks the destination 'to/up into the sky' (بر + آسمان); بر همای places the word همای (the mythical roc-like bird) after the verb in inverted/emphatic order — effectively 'the Homay did not fly to the sky.' The repetition of بر is idiomatic emphasis and the subject is postposed for poetic effect.
